evidence: Datacenter and communications was 72% of Q2 revenue ($1.208B) and management tied H2 FY2026 and FY2027 growth to continued datacenter demand plus production capacity expansion.
Q3 guide calls for $1.70B-$1.84B revenue and 38.5%-40.5% non-GAAP gross margin, implying AI-optics demand is supporting both growth and pricing/mix.

March 2026 NVIDIA partnership added a multibillion-dollar purchase commitment, future capacity rights for advanced laser and optical networking products, and a $2B NVIDIA investment to expand Coherent's U.S. manufacturing and R&D base.

OFC 2026 demos included 6.4T socketed silicon-photonics CPO, VCSEL-based socketed CPO, and a 400G-per-lane InP-on-silicon modulator path, showing Coherent has multiple technical routes into future package-level optics.
Revenue +168% YoY, backlog 4x'd to $66.8B, 'unable to catch up with demand signals', all capacity booked through Aug-Sep 2026
Delivered 50K GB200s in Q4 to single customer. Nvidia GPUs 'remain in short supply' per Intrator. Massive capex plan ($30-35B) = enormous chip demand.
Every GPU CRWV deploys needs HBM. $30-35B capex = substantial HBM demand. Storage ARR >$100M and growing.
850 MW active, 3.1 GW contracted, targeting 5+ GW by 2030. 43 data centers. 260 MW added in Q4 alone.
Every NVL72 rack deployed uses pluggable optics for scale-out. 100K+ GPUs deployed in Q4 = significant transceiver demand.
Intel said AI-driven demand created meaningful supply constraints, DCAI revenue rose 15% sequentially, and revenue would have been materially higher with more supply.
Intel Foundry is ramping 18A, raising EUV wafer mix, and engaging external customers on 14A, which is precisely the kind of alternative leading-edge supply response that could ease the crunch later.
Intel flagged rising DRAM, NAND, and substrate pricing due to intense AI infrastructure demand, showing upstream component constraints are now feeding through into Intel's model.
Revenue rose 65.5% YoY, components were 66.7% of total revenue, and Q3 guide points to another major step up, showing AI optical demand is already moving the reported P&L.
OCS backlog is already above $400M, and the R64 OCS extends Lumentum's optical-circuit-switch lineup into lower-port-count AI deployments with 72x72 scaling and major power savings.

Management disclosed an incremental multi-hundred-million-dollar CPO order for 1H 2027 delivery, while NVIDIA added a $2B investment, a multibillion purchase commitment, and future capacity rights for advanced laser components.
Management said DRAM and NAND data-center bit TAM should exceed 50% of industry TAM in 2026 and that both AI and traditional server demand are constrained by inadequate supply.
Record Q2 results with 74.9% non-GAAP gross margin and a Q3 guide of $33.5B revenue / ~81% gross margin show the shortage is converting directly into pricing and earnings power.
Micron has begun HBM4 volume shipments for NVIDIA Vera Rubin and is investing above $25B in FY2026 capex, reinforcing that memory capacity is being pulled hard by the 2026 accelerator ramp.

Rubin moving to N3, competing with TPU v7/v8, Trainium3, MI350X for TSMC capacity. $95.2B supply commitments (nearly doubled QoQ) = enormous wafer demand. Vera Rubin production H2 2026 adds further N3 pressure. Six new chips this cycle — all need N3.

$21.4B inventory (significant HBM stockpile). Every GPU shipped needs HBM. NVIDIA GPU volumes are primary demand driver for memory supercycle. CRWV alone ordering $30-35B capex worth of GPUs = massive HBM pull-through.

Networking revenue $11B (+3.5x YoY). Every NVL72 rack uses OSFP pluggable transceivers. Spectrum-X "a home run." "We ship racks of computers" = optics in every unit. Grace Blackwell = ~2/3 of DC revenue — all rack-scale with optics.
Supply commitments doubled ($50.3B → $95.2B). $500B revenue visibility. Demand "growing exponentially." Validates CRWV thesis — NVIDIA can't build fast enough.
Q4 revenue reached $33.73B with gross margin 62.3%, and management said both Q4 and Q1 2026 were supported by strong demand for leading-edge process technologies.
3nm already accounted for 28% of wafer revenue, and 7nm-and-below advanced nodes were 77% of total wafer revenue, showing AI demand remains concentrated in constrained leading-edge supply.
A 2026 capital budget of $52B-$56B is a real supply response. It does not break the shortage today, but it is exactly the kind of capex that can eventually ease logic tightness.

Wang draws LNG market parallel: when buyers shifted from spot to 15-20yr take-or-pay contracts, price volatility compressed and suppliers re-rated from cyclical to quasi-utility (Cheniere, Shell, TotalEnergies). HBM contracting trending same direction. If analogy holds, 5-8x fwd P/E on memory embeds unwarranted cyclical discount. SK Hynix 68.8% GM, Micron 74.9% GM — approaching/exceeding TSMC for first time in history.

Wang's margin-as-residual framework. NVIDIA captures 75%+ of GPU ASP while contributing ~14% of BoM. Margin absorbs pressure from TSMC (5-10% annual hikes), HBM oligopoly (20%+ price increases), and hyperscaler custom silicon (TPU v7, Trainium2/3, Maia 200 as credible BATNA). GAAP GM already 75.0% → 71.1%. Street models 74% → 71% over 5yr, likely understating bilateral pressure magnitude.

Wang ranks CoWoS as #1 in substitutability ranking (most constrained, least substitutable). CoWoS capacity ~75K wafers/month early 2026, targeting 130K by late 2026, but demand at 113% CAGR keeps it fully booked. TSMC at ~25x fwd P/E is a discount to S&P 500 IT (~32x) despite 92% sub-5nm share and packaging monopoly.
